name: colorForth
summary: Chuck Moore's idiosyncratic Forth: source representation is color-tagged 32-bit cells (4-bit token class + 28-bit symbol). The on-disk file *is* the parsed AST. Tiny subroutine-threaded compiler that inlines several primitive arithmetic operations. Source notation, compiler size, and machine model are co-designed.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**colorForth** (Chuck Moore) is the most idiosyncratic line in the Forth tradition: a color-tagged source representation paired with a tiny subroutine-threaded compiler that inlines several primitive arithmetic operations. The editor stores source as 32-bit cells where the upper 4 bits encode token class (color-coded in display) and the lower 28 bits encode the symbol. The on-disk file *is* the parsed AST.

There is no surface text-to-tokens pass; the editor produces tokens directly, and the compiler is a trivial walker over the pre-parsed cells. This is the most extreme "representation as ground truth" in the Forth tradition: the program form on disk is the same form the compiler consumes.

sections.title: Co-design — source, compiler, machine model
sections.content:
colorForth is interesting because source notation, compiler size, and machine model are co-designed. The arrayForth derivative for the GreenArrays GA144 chip takes this further — the chip's 18-bit instruction-slot constraints inform the source representation directly.

For a language designer, colorForth is the data point on "what happens if you co-design source representation with the compiler and target hardware?" The answer: dramatic compactness, fast compilation, and a development environment that fits in a few hundred lines. The cost is that the design is so unconventional it doesn't compose with anything else; colorForth is its own ecosystem.
